name: Fast and Furious Scandal
description: The Fast and Furious scandal, officially known as Operation Fast and Furious, was a controversial law enforcement operation run by the Bureau of Alcohol, Tobacco, Firearms and Explosives (ATF) from 2009 to 2011. The operation aimed to track weapons purchased in the United States and smuggled into Mexico, with the intention of identifying and dismantling Mexican drug cartels. The controversy arose when it was revealed that the ATF knowingly allowed straw purchasers to buy firearms with the intention of allowing them to be trafficked across the border, rather than interdicting the weapons at the point of sale. Proponents of conspiracy theories surrounding Fast and Furious often claim that the operation was intentionally designed to fail, with the ultimate goal of justifying stricter gun control legislation in the United States. Some theories posit that high-ranking government officials were aware of and approved the operation, and that the operation resulted in the deaths of U.S. Border Patrol Agent Brian Terry and potentially hundreds of Mexican citizens. Mainstream investigations, including those by the Department of Justice and Congress, have acknowledged serious flaws and misjudgments in the operation's execution, but have largely refuted claims of a deliberate plot to arm cartels or undermine the Second Amendment. The Justice Department's Inspector General concluded that the ATF made significant errors in judgment, failed to adequately supervise the operation, and provided misleading information to Mexican authorities. Congressional investigations focused on identifying those responsible for the flawed strategies and lack of oversight. While the operation's failures are well-documented, the more extreme conspiracy theories surrounding Fast and Furious remain largely unsubstantiated and rely on circumstantial evidence and speculation.
subtitle: Controversial gunwalking operation by the ATF

claims.claim: The ATF intentionally allowed guns to reach Mexican cartels to justify stricter gun control.
claims.evidence: The ATF did allow straw purchasers to buy guns that were then trafficked to Mexico. The operation was poorly planned and executed, leading to guns ending up in the hands of criminals.
claims.criticism: Mainstream investigations found serious errors in judgment and oversight by the ATF, but no evidence of a deliberate plot to arm cartels or undermine the Second Amendment. The goal was to trace weapons, however flawed the execution.

claims.claim: High-ranking government officials, including President Obama and Attorney General Eric Holder, were directly involved in planning and approving Fast and Furious.
claims.evidence: Some documents and testimonies suggest that Holder was aware of the operation, and some believe the White House was aware as well. The timing of the operation coincided with increased calls for gun control.
claims.criticism: Multiple investigations found no direct evidence linking Obama or Holder to the planning or approval of Fast and Furious. While Holder was criticized for his department's handling of the investigation, no evidence of direct involvement was found.

claims.claim: Fast and Furious was a 'false flag' operation designed to incite violence and destabilize Mexico, creating a pretext for U.S. intervention.
claims.evidence: Some argue that the operation intentionally created chaos in Mexico, potentially benefiting certain political or economic interests in the US.
claims.criticism: This claim is highly speculative and lacks concrete evidence. While the operation did contribute to violence in Mexico, there is no credible evidence to suggest it was intentionally designed to do so as a pretext for intervention.

keyFigures.name: Dennis Burke
keyFigures.role: Former U.S. Attorney for the District of Arizona, oversaw the operation

keyFigures.name: Darrell Issa
keyFigures.role: Former U.S. Representative, led congressional investigations into Fast and Furious

keyFigures.name: Sharyl Attkisson
keyFigures.role: Investigative journalist who reported extensively on the scandal

keyFigures.name: Brian Terry
keyFigures.role: U.S. Border Patrol Agent killed in a shootout where weapons from Fast and Furious were recovered

timeline.event: Border Patrol Agent Brian Terry is killed
Fast and Furious becomes public knowledge and sparks controversy
Attorney General Eric Holder is held in contempt of Congress for refusing to release documents related to the operation

sources.author: U.S. Department of Justice, Office of the Inspector General

sources.title: U.S. Department of Justice, Office of the Inspector General
